coredata.dc:title: The acid modified magnetic carbon quantum dots as a capable nano-catalyst for the synthesis of pyridines and pyrazoles
coredata.dc:creator: Ghorbani, Shiva
Habibi, Davood
Heydari, Somayyeh
Ebrahimiasl, Hakimeh
coredata.dc:description: The acid modified magnetic carbon quantum dots [Fe3O4@CQDs@Si(CH2)3NH@CC@TET@ SO3H] was easily prepared, characterized by the FT-IR, VSM, TEM, SEM, TGA, XRD, and EDX techniques and used as a capable heterogeneous nano-catalyst in a multicomponent condensation reactions for the synthesis of two sets of heterocycles: a) pyridines and b) pyrazoles in high yields and very short reaction times. Also, the prepared new nano-catalyst easily separated from the reaction mixture by a magnet and showed a good catalytic stability and used in six consecutive runs without the significant loss of catalytic activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Acid modified magnetic CQDs
Nano-catalysis
MCRs
Pyridines
Pyrazoles

head.ce:title: The acid modified magnetic carbon quantum dots as a capable nano-catalyst for the synthesis of pyridines and pyrazoles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Organic Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Bu-Ali Sina University, Hamedan 6517838683, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Organic Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Bu-Ali Sina University, Hamedan 6517838683, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Saveh University of Medical Sciences, Saveh, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Saveh University of Medical Sciences, Saveh, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The acid modified magnetic carbon quantum dots [Fe3O4@CQDs@Si(CH2)3NH@CC@TET@ SO3H] was easily prepared, characterized by the FT-IR, VSM, TEM, SEM, TGA, XRD, and EDX techniques and used as a capable heterogeneous nano-catalyst in a multicomponent condensation reactions for the synthesis of two sets of heterocycles: a) pyridines and b) pyrazoles in high yields and very short reaction times. Also, the prepared new nano-catalyst easily separated from the reaction mixture by a magnet and showed a good catalytic stability and used in six consecutive runs without the significant loss of catalytic activity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: One of the most important goals of organic chemists is to find ways to make processes simple, and environmentally friendly in one-step reaction. In this field, the use of nontoxic and recyclable catalysts has been proposed and their catalytic activities studied in various reactions involving formation of the new carbon-heteroatom and the carbon–carbon bonds [1–3]. Among the two main groups of catalysts (homogeneous and heterogeneous), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have interesting advantageous, since they are highly soluble, very active and stable during the reaction time [4]. CQDs have some drawbacks and the only way to recycle them is their deactivation at the end of the reaction, however, this process produces a lot of useless waste [5], and apparently application of nanoparticles is an important way to get rid of this problem.

Fe3O4 MNPs, modified with organic and inorganic compounds have recently attracted huge attention [6–12]. Today, many methods have been reported for the synthesis of magnetic nano-particles in different shapes and textures. The use of nano-catalysts result in a higher reaction rate due to their high surface area, but since they have a great tendency to aggregate, the most important thing in this regard is to maintain their stability. Being highly active and easy to separate, the nanoparticles have been utilized in various industries, as well as in drug delivery, and in multicomponent reaction, as well [13–15]. To keep the nanoparticles stable during the reaction, various solutions such as using surfactant, silica, soaking them in a matrix/support and carbon coating have been reported [16–18]. Fe3O4 MNPs have many benefits in organic reactions, a) lower catalyst filtration compared to other supported catalysts; b) production is simple, cheap, cost-effective, scalable, and controllable; c) simple separation using magnets, eliminating the need for additional chemicals or filtration; d) stable catalytic bonds allow for environmentally friendly solvents; and e) Fe3O4 MNPs are inexpensive and non-toxic [19–22].

From a green chemistry perspective, it is of great importance to design and create economic chemical processes using heterogeneous catalysts for multi-component reaction (MCR) and chemical and pharmaceutical production [23–25]. Multicomponent reactions have significant advantages such as high atom economy and bond formation efficiency, avoiding the production of by-products, fast and easy implementation, time and energy savings, and avoidance of complex purification steps [26].

Some pyridine and pyrazole containing heterocycles have exhibited significant bio/ pharmacological characteristics; they are analgesic, anti-inflammatory, anti-bacterial, anti-pyretic, anti-microbial, and they are helpful with cardiovascular conditions [27–32].

In this study, acid modified magnetic CQDs was prepared, characterized and used as a capable new nanocatalyst for the synthesis of the two sets of the following heterocycles:
i.
pyridines a(1–12) and
ii.
pyrazoles b(1–12), with excellent performance.
The new catalyst is inexpensive, efficient, environmentally friendly with easy separation, and being able to be used multiple times without the significat loss of activity.

All reagents were purchased from the foreign chemical companies and used as received. Melting points were measured in open capillary tubes using a BUCHI 510 apparatus. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded in KBr pellets on a Perkin Elmer GX FT‐IR spectrometer. The NMR spectra were recorded on 250 and 400 MHz BRUKER AVANCE instruments in DSMO-d6 . Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) images were obtained on an KYKY EM3200 instrument operated at 30 kV accelerating voltage. The high resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) was conducted using a HRTEM Philips CM30, (300KV) instrument. Energy-Dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis was carried out using a FESEM-SIGM (German) instrument. The Thermo Gravimetric Analysis (TGA) were performed by TGA PYRIS DIAMOND. Magnetic measurements of the new catalyst were performed using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) instrument. The ICP mesausrments were carried out by an Inductively Coupled Plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP‐OES).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the acid-modified magnetic CQD nano-catalyst
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of acid modified magnetic carbon quantum dots Fe3O4@CQDs@Si(CH2)3NH@CC @TET@SO3H was perfomed in the following six stages:

Fe3O4MNPs (A) was prepared according to a literature [33]. Briefly, FeCl3·6H2O (0.02 mol) and FeCl2·4H2O (0.01 mol) were dissolved in water (80 mL) and the pH of the mixture adjusted at about 10 by adddition of the 37 % ammonia solution (10 mL) and heated at 80 °C under the N2 atmosphere for 0.5 h. Fe3O4MNPs (A), as a black solid was separated by a magnet, washed with water and air dried.

Edible oil (20 mL) was slowly heated until it reached to 250 °C, and then, glucose (10 g) was added and heated at this temperature for 10 min until it turned completely black. The mixture was cooled to room temperature, the equal amounts of water and diethyl ether (20 mL) added and the synthesized quantum carbon dot separated by decanting. Separation was repeated several times until all the synthesized carbon quantum dot enters the aqueous phase and the oil enters the organic phase [34]. Then, Fe3O4MNPs (A) (1 g) was dispersed with ultrasonication in deionized water (60 mL) for 15 min, and the prepared CQDs (0.05 g) added and stirred for 24 h at room temperature. Fe3O4@CQD (B) was separated using a magnet and washed several times with distilled water and oven dried at 60 °C.

Fe3O4@CQD (B) (1 g) was dispersed in toluene by ultrasonication for 15 min and (3-amino-propyl)triethoxysilane (2 mL) slowly added to the mixture and refluxed for 24 h at 110 °C. Fe3O4@CQDs@Si(CH2)3NH2 (C) was separated by a magnet, washed with toluene and oven dried at 60 °C.

Cyanuric chloride (CC, 6 g, 32 mmol) was stirred in dry THF (50 mL) for 3 h at 0 °C under the nitrogen atmosphere. Then, Fe3O4@CQDs@Si(CH2)3NH2 C (1 g) was added and stirred further at room temperature for 24 h. Fe3O4@CQDs@Si(CH2)3NH@CC (D) was separated by an external magnet, washed by THF and ethanol and oven dried at 60 °C.

Fe3O4@CQDs@Si(CH2)3NH@CC (D) (1 g), 5-aminotetrazole (TET, 24 mmol, 2.04 g) and potassium carbonate (24 mmol, 3.3 g) were dissolved in toluene (70 mL) and the mixture refluxed overnight. Then, Fe3O4@CQDs@Si(CH2)3NH@CC@TET (E) was separated by a magnet washed with toluene and oven dried at 60 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of acid modified magnetic carbon quantum dots Fe3O4@CQDs@Si(CH2)3NH@ CC@TET@SO3H (F)
ce:section.ce:para: In the flask number one, Fe3O4@CQDs@Si(CH2)3NH@CC@TET (E) (1 g) was dispersed in dry CH2Cl2 (20 mL) by ultrasonication for 15 min. In the flask number two, chlorosulfonic acid (0.3 mL) was added to CH2Cl2 (20 mL), and its content added dropwise to the content of the flask number one, and stirred at 0 °C until all the gas came out and stirred further at room temperature for 24 h. Fe3O4@CQDs@Si(CH2)3NH@CC@TET@SO3H (F) was separated by a magnet, washed with dry CH2Cl2 and oven dried at 60 °C (Scheme 1 ).

A ketone (1 mmol), ammonium acetate (1 mmol), aromatic aldehyde (1 mmol), malononitrile (1 mmol), and F (10 mg) was reacted in solvent-free condition at 80 °C. After completion of the reaction (TLC), the mixture was cooled, F separated by a magnet and the product characterized with FT-IR, 1H and 13C NMR (Scheme 2 ).

An aromatic aldehyde (1 mmol), phenyl hydrazine (1 mmol), malononitrile (1 mmol) and F (15 mg) was reacted in solvent-free condition at 70 °C. After completion of the reaction (TLC), the mixture was cooled, the nanocatalyst separated by a magnet FT-IR, 1H and 13C NMR spectra were determined by spectra in deuterated solvents (Scheme 3 ).
Structure of F was characterized by FT-IR, XRD, SEM, TEM, TGA-DTA, EDX, and VSM analysis.

Fig. 1 displays the IR spectra of A, B, C, D, E, and F. The characteristic peaks of A at 577 cm−1 was displayed in A–F. The two peaks at about 3391 and 1703 cm−1 are related to the OH and CO bond (B–F). The three peaks at about 3454, 2922 and 1061 cm−1 belong to the NH, CH, and SiO in trimethoxysilyl propylamine, respectively (C). The peak at about 1614 cm−1 belongs to the CN bond confirming the successful attendance of the cyanuric chloride group in D. The two peaks at about 1551 and 1615 cm−1 are related to the CN and NN bonds (E) confirming the presence of the 5-aminotetrazole skeleton. Finally, the peak at about 1062 cm−1 (F) is assigned to the SO stretching bond confirming the presence of the SO3H group.

The XRD patterns (Fig. 2 ) confirm the crystal structures of A and F. As shown, the positions and relative intensities of all the prepared F diffraction peaks match relatively the standard XRD pattern A (JCPDS No. 65-3107) [35]. They also show the cubic iron oxide structure corresponding to the (111), (220), (311), (400), (422), (440) and (531) planes. From the XRD pattern, the average crystallite size of NPs can be calculated using the Debye-Scherrer equation (D = Kλ/βcosθ).
Eventually, the Bragg equation (dhkl = λ/2sinθ) was applied to obtain the distance between the layers (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization by EDX and elemental mappings
ce:section.ce:para: The EDX analysis was performed to show the presence of the expected elements (C, Fe, Si, S, O, and N) in the acid modified magnetic carbon quantum dots. Fig. 3 shows the successful formation of the nano-catalyst and corresponding elemental mapping analysis indicates their homogeneous distributions, as well.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization by the SEM and TEM images
ce:section.ce:para: The SEM image of F (Fig. 4 ) shows the morphological structure and particle distribution of F indicating that the size of the particles is in the range of 30–44 nm.
The TEM image of F (Fig. 5 ) shows that these nanoparticles are quasi-spherical in shape and have an almost uniform particle size distribution with a core–shell structure.

Fig. 6 display the magnetization of A, E, and F (78, 54, and 49 emu/g, respectively). Decrease in the magnetic values from A to F could be attributed to the gradual coating of MNPs by the CQDs, Si(CH2)3NH2, CC, TET, and SO3H layers, but they still have considerable magnetic properties to be separated from the reaction mixture.
Fig. 7 shows the TGA curve of F to find information about its thermal stability and performance.

The first weight loss at about 110 °C is related to the desorption of water or other organic solvents. The second weight loss from 300 to 450 °C is probably referred to the decomposition of organic layers, and the third mass loss after 480 °C is due to the complete decomposition of nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the reaction conditions for the synthesis of a1
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic activity of F was investigated for the synthesis of a1 in the four-component condensation reaction of acetophenone, ammonium acetate, benzaldehyde, and malononitrile (model reaction) in different solvents, temperatures and various amounts of F (Table 2 ). The best result was found to be the 1:1:1:1 mol ratio of acetophenone, ammonium acetate, benzaldehyde, and malononitrile with 10 mg of F at 80 °C in solvent-free conditions with high yield (93 %) and very short reaction time (10 min).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of pyridines a(1–12) under the optimal conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the obtained optimal results from the model reaction, diverse pyridines were synthesized with good to high yields and very short reaction times (Table 3 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The proposed mechanism for the synthesis of pyridines a(1–12)
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed mechanism for the synthesis of pyridines by F is suggested in Fig. 8 .
In pathway A, the carbonyl group of an aldehyde is activated by F to be susceptible for the nucleophhilic attack of imine form of malononitrile and subsequent elimination of water to form A.
In pathway B, the carbonyl group of a phenone is activated by F to undergo the nucleophhilic attack of amonia and subsequent deletion of water to form B.
Then, nucleophilic attack of B on A forms C, which with subsequent internal cyclization and elimination of H2 forms the final product.
Table 4 shows the comparison of F with other catalysts reported in literature for the synthesis of pyridines to indicate the capability of F in terms of time and efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the reaction conditions for the synthesis of b1
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic activity of F was studied for the synthesis of b1 in the three-components condensation reaction of a phenylhydrazine, benzaldehyde, and malononitrile (model reaction) in different solvents, temperatures and amounts of the catalyst (Table 5 ). The best result was found to be the 1:1:1 mol ratio of phenylhydrazine (1 mmol), benzaldehyde (1 mmol), and malononitrile with 15 mg of F at 70 °C in solvent-free conditions with high yield (95 %) and very short reaction time (7 min).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of pyrazoles b(1–12) under the optimal conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the obtained optimal results from the model reaction, diverse pyrazoles were synthesized with good to high yields and very short reaction times (Table 6 ).
Yield: 89 %, mp: Semi-solid. IR (KBr): ν = 3343, 2955, 2232, 1401 and 1101 cm−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The proposed mechanism for the synthesis of pyrazoles b(1–12) by F
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed mechanism for the synthesis of pyrazoles by F is suggested in Fig. 9 . The carbonyl group of an aldehyde is activated by F to be susceptible for the nucleophhilic attack of imine form of malononitrile with elimination of water to form (I). Then, the nucleophhilic attack of phenylhydrazine on (I) froms (II) which the internal cyclization and elimination of water forms the final prodcut.

Recyclability of F was investigated in two following sets of reactions in the optimal conditions:
i)
the synthesis of pyridines (blue), and
ii)
the synthesis of pyrazoles (green) in the 6 consecutive runs which the results almost show no significant decrease in the catalyst activity (Fig. 10 ).
Table 7 shows the comparison of F with other catalysts reported in literature for the synthesis of pyrazoles to indicate the capability of F in terms of time and efficiency.

The purpose of this research was preparation and characterization of a novel and capable nano catalyst and its use for the synthesis of two types of compounds with probable biological activities:
(i)
Pyridines a(1–12) from the one-pot four component condensation reaction of aldehydes, malononitrile, acetophenone, and ammonium acetate at 80 °C, and
(ii)
Pyrazoles b(1–12) from the one-pot three component condensation reaction of aldehydes, malononitrile, and phenylhydrazine at 70 °C.
Easy separation, recyclability of the new catalyst after several runs, high yield, short reaction time, and the use of solvent-free conditions (green solvent), are advantages of the proposed method.
Shiva Ghorbani: Project administration. Davood Habibi:Project administration. Somayyeh Heydari: Project administration. Hakimeh Ebrahimiasl: Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper. No funds.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: First report of the application of simple molecular complexes as organo-catalysts for Knoevenagel condensation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.K. Panja, N. Dwivedi, S. Saha, First report of the application of simple molecular complexes as organo-catalysts for Knoevenagel condensation. RSC Adv., 5 (2015) 65526 -65531.
A.F. Trindade, P.M. Gois, C.A. Afonso, Recyclable stereoselective catalysts. Chem. Rev., 109 (2009) 418-514.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recyclable catalysts for the synthesis of heterocyclic compounds using carbon materials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Azarifar, H. Ebrahimiasl, R. Karamian, M. Ahmadi-Khoei, s-Triazinium-based ionic liquid immobilized on silica-coated Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles: An efficient and magnetically separable heterogeneous catalyst for synthesis of 2-amino-4,8-dihydropyrano -[3,2-b]pyran-3-carbonitrile derivatives for antioxidant and antifungal evaluation studies.J. Iran. Chem. Soc.,16 (2019) 341-354.

Chem., 34 (2020) e5769.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ebrahimiasl, D. Azarifar, Copper‐based Schiff base complex immobilized on core‐shell Fe3O4@SiO2 as a magnetically recyclable and highly efficient nanocatalyst for green synthesis of 2‐amino‐4H‐chromenes.Appl. Organomet. Chem., 34 (2020) e5359.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: gC3N4 quantum dot decorated MoS2/Fe3O4 as a novel recoverable catalyst for photodegradation of organic pollutant under visible light

Appl. Organomet. Chem., 34 (2020) e5547.

Eng. C., 98 (2019) 19-29.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A magnetic metal–organic framework as a highly active heterogeneous catalyst for one-pot synthesis of 2-substituted alkyl and aryl (indolyl) kojic acid derivatives
